category_title: Elementary-aged children with mild/moderate autism and significant language delays
minimal_context: The student and a peer are playing with grocery store toys on the carpet in a sectioned-off hallway.
dialogue:
TUTOR: We are going to play with the grocery store toys on the carpet for ten minutes. You and your partner can play together.

STUDENT: (Picks up a plastic apple) Red apple.

TUTOR: Your partner has the shopping basket.

STUDENT: (Looks at the basket) Basket.

TUTOR: What can you say to your partner?

STUDENT: (Reaches for the basket without asking) Put apple.

options.A: Use a visual chart for checking in where specific numbers, colors, and faces related to anxiety are matched with feeling words during the ten-minute play session.
options.B: Sit away from the play area, watch the children, and use verbal re-directs and reflective statements to sustain the play interaction, without prompting or commenting when the dyad is engaged.
options.C: Have more new motivating grocery store toys handy to introduce to the student and peer during the ten-minute play session on the carpet.
options.D: Work on the recognition of body language and facial cues with the student and peer while, at the same time, labeling feelings during the ten-minute play session.
